title: Introduction to Java Streams
body:
Java Streams (Java 8+) provide a declarative, functional-style API for processing sequences of elements. A stream represents a pipeline of operations applied to a data source.

Key concepts:
- **Source** — collection, array, file, generator
- **Intermediate operations** — lazy, return a stream: `filter`, `map`, `sorted`, `distinct`, `limit`, `skip`, `peek`
- **Terminal operations** — trigger evaluation: `collect`, `forEach`, `reduce`, `count`, `findFirst`, `anyMatch`

Streams are **lazy** — intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called, and only the elements needed are processed.

Streams are **single-use** — once a terminal operation is called, the stream is consumed and cannot be reused.

Use parallel streams (`parallelStream()`) for CPU-bound operations on large datasets, but be careful with shared mutable state.

examples.label: Basic stream pipeline

title: The Java Stream API Tutorial
body:
The Stream API offers a rich set of operations for data transformation.

**Mapping:**
- `map(fn)` — transform each element
- `flatMap(fn)` — flatten nested streams
- `mapToInt/Long/Double` — convert to primitive streams

**Filtering/Slicing:**
- `filter(predicate)` — keep matching elements
- `distinct()` — remove duplicates
- `limit(n)` / `skip(n)` — take/skip elements
- `takeWhile(p)` / `dropWhile(p)` — Java 9+

**Reducing:**
- `reduce(identity, accumulator)` — fold elements
- `count()`, `min()`, `max()`
- `sum()`, `average()` — on numeric streams

**Collecting:**
- `collect(Collectors.toList())` — to list
- `collect(Collectors.groupingBy(fn))` — grouping
- `collect(Collectors.joining(", "))` — string join

**Matching:**
- `anyMatch()`, `allMatch()`, `noneMatch()` — boolean predicates
- `findFirst()`, `findAny()` — Optional result

examples.label: flatMap and groupingBy

title: Functional Interfaces in Java
body:
Functional interfaces are the building blocks of the Stream API and lambda expressions. Each defines a single abstract method (SAM).

The most common interfaces in `java.util.function`:

**`Function<T, R>`** — `R apply(T t)` — transform T to R. Chain with `andThen()` or `compose()`.

**`Predicate<T>`** — `boolean test(T t)` — condition check. Combine with `and()`, `or()`, `negate()`.

**`Consumer<T>`** — `void accept(T t)` — side effect. Chain with `andThen()`.

**`Supplier<T>`** — `T get()` — produce a value lazily.

**`UnaryOperator<T>`** — `T apply(T t)` — same type in and out.

**`BiFunction<T,U,R>`** — `R apply(T t, U u)` — two input types.

Method references (`Class::method`) are shorthand for lambda expressions.

examples.label: Predicate composition

title: Guide to Java Collectors
body:
`Collectors` is a utility class providing implementations of the `Collector` interface used with `Stream.collect()`.

**Collecting to collections:**
- `toList()` — to mutable List
- `toUnmodifiableList()` — immutable
- `toSet()` — to HashSet
- `toMap(keyFn, valueFn)` — to HashMap
- `toCollection(supplier)` — to any collection

**Grouping and partitioning:**
- `groupingBy(classifier)` — Map<K, List<T>>
- `groupingBy(classifier, downstream)` — nested collectors
- `partitioningBy(predicate)` — Map<Boolean, List<T>>

**Summarizing:**
- `counting()` — count of elements
- `summingInt/Long/Double(fn)` — sum
- `averagingDouble(fn)` — average
- `summarizingInt(fn)` — min, max, sum, count, average

**Joining:**
- `joining()` — concatenate strings
- `joining(delimiter, prefix, suffix)`

examples.label: groupingBy with downstream collector

title: Java Stream Filter with Lambda Expression
body:
`Stream.filter(predicate)` is an intermediate operation that keeps only elements matching the given predicate.

```java
stream.filter(e -> e.getSalary() > 50000)
```

The predicate is any `Predicate<T>` — a lambda, method reference, or composed predicate.

**Filtering with multiple conditions:**
```java
.filter(e -> e.getSalary() > 50000 && e.getDept().equals("Engineering"))
// or using Predicate composition:
Predicate<Employee> highPaid = e -> e.getSalary() > 50000;
Predicate<Employee> isEngineer = e -> e.getDept().equals("Engineering");
.filter(highPaid.and(isEngineer))
```

**Filtering nulls:**
```java
.filter(Objects::nonNull)
```

`filter` is lazy — it only evaluates the predicate when the terminal operation demands the next element. This enables short-circuit evaluation with `findFirst()` or `anyMatch()`.

examples.label: Filter with Predicate composition
